sections.heading: Surgery
sections.content:
Surgery is only used for select cases of vaginal cancer. For the majority of vaginal cancers, radiation is used rather than surgery as primary treatment due to improved organ preservation. Surgery is recommended only if a complete resection with clear margins is feasible without excessive morbidity and with likelihood that no adjuvant RT would be required. Patients should be evaluated by a gynecologic oncologist prior to any surgical treatment for vaginal cancer.

Principles:
- Surgery is only recommended if a complete resection with clear margins is feasible without excessive morbidity and with likelihood that no adjuvant RT would be required
- For primary, untreated lesions in which resection would require excision of the urethra, bladder, or rectum, radiation is often preferred
- Vaginectomy may be considered for small lesions for which margins are likely to be negative. Every effort should be made to obtain negative margins
- For microscopic lesions at the top of the vagina, upper vaginectomy ± hysterectomy may be reasonable
- A radical hysterectomy may be appropriate for macroscopic lesions (<2 cm)
- For proximal lesions involving the upper two thirds of the vagina, the pelvic lymph nodes should be assessed
- For distal lesions involving the lower one third of the vagina, the inguinal lymph nodes should be assessed
- Vaginal reconstruction should be considered for appropriate candidates desiring such procedures
- In patients who are premenopausal, ovarian preservation or transposition should be considered when feasible
- Fiducial markers may be placed to define the extent of the vaginal lesion
- EUA may be helpful to confirm diagnosis, obtain adequate tissue sampling for histologic evaluation and comprehensive molecular profiling such as PD-L1, and assess the extent of disease. Consider cystoscopy and proctoscopy concurrently to exclude bladder/rectal invasion. Perform evaluation of cervix and vulva to exclude other gynecologic primary sites

Resectability Criteria:
Surgery is only recommended if a complete resection with clear margins is feasible without excessive morbidity and with likelihood that no adjuvant RT would be required. For primary untreated lesions in which resection would require excision of the urethra, bladder, or rectum, radiation is often preferred. Pelvic exenteration may be considered for recurrent or persistent disease localized to the pelvis or when primary RT is not feasible.

Pre-operative Optimisation:
Patients should be evaluated by a gynecologic oncologist prior to any surgical treatment. EUA with biopsies, cystoscopy, and proctoscopy should be performed to assess the extent of disease and exclude bladder/rectal invasion. Fiducial markers may be placed to define the extent of the vaginal lesion. In patients who are premenopausal, ovarian preservation or transposition should be considered when feasible.

cards.name: Upper vaginectomy ± hysterectomy
cards.detail: Approach: Surgical excision. Indications: Microscopic lesions at the top of the vagina. Key complications: Adhesion formation, pain, potential small bowel obstruction, urinary or gastrointestinal complications, pelvic floor dysfunction, lymphedema. Operative mortality: Not specified.

cards.name: Radical hysterectomy
cards.detail: Approach: Surgical excision. Indications: Macroscopic lesions <2 cm confined to vagina. Key complications: Adhesion formation, pain, potential small bowel obstruction, urinary or gastrointestinal complications, pelvic floor dysfunction, lymphedema. Operative mortality: Not specified.

cards.name: Partial or radical vaginectomy
cards.detail: Approach: Surgical excision. Indications: Small lesions for which margins are likely to be negative. Key complications: Adhesion formation, pain, potential small bowel obstruction, urinary or gastrointestinal complications, pelvic floor dysfunction, lymphedema. Operative mortality: Not specified.

cards.name: Pelvic exenteration
cards.detail: Approach: Radical surgical excision ± intraoperative RT (IORT). Indications: Recurrent or persistent disease localized to the pelvis, or when primary RT is not feasible. Central pelvic recurrent disease after prior EBRT and/or brachytherapy. Key complications: Adhesion formation, pain, potential small bowel obstruction, urinary or gastrointestinal complications, pelvic floor dysfunction, lymphedema. Concomitant measures include adequate rehabilitation programs dealing with psychosocial and psychosexual consequences as well as reconstructive procedures. Operative mortality: Generally ≤5%.
